summary: Microsoft documents SubmitSamplesConsent on the Defender Spynet policy path. In Win25H2Clean, SecurityHealthService.exe read SubmitSamplesConsent = 2 directly from the policy path after the write.

setting.name: Microsoft Defender Sample Submission
setting.casual_explanation: Microsoft Defender can send file samples to Microsoft when cloud analysis needs them. This policy controls whether Defender prompts, sends safe samples, never sends, or sends all samples automatically.
setting.why_people_change_it: People change this when they want tighter control over file-sample uploads or when they are comparing Defender cloud-protection behavior.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Lowering sample submission can reduce data sent to Microsoft, but it can also weaken Block at First Sight and other cloud-backed protection paths.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents SubmitSamplesConsent on HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Spynet with 0 = Always prompt, 1 = Send safe samples automatically, 2 = Never send, and 3 = Send all samples automatically. Microsoft also states in the Block at First Sight policy notes that value 2 means the feature will not function. In the Win25H2Clean VM, SecurityHealthService.exe read SubmitSamplesConsent = 2 directly from the Defender Spynet policy path after the write.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Leave the sample-submission policy unset and let Defender use its managed baseline.

allowed_values.label: Always prompt
allowed_values.meaning: Defender prompts before sending samples for cloud analysis.

allowed_values.label: Send safe samples automatically
allowed_values.meaning: Defender sends safe samples automatically and keeps the documented default behavior.

allowed_values.label: Never send
allowed_values.meaning: Defender does not send samples automatically. Microsoft notes this disables Block at First Sight.

allowed_values.label: Send all samples automatically
allowed_values.meaning: Defender sends all samples automatically for cloud analysis.

targets.notes: The runtime proof is strong for value 2. This is a documented protection tradeoff, not an unknown value model.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes SubmitSamplesConsent = 2 on the documented Defender Spynet policy path.

windows_defaults.label: Policy unset baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Systems where the Defender sample-submission policy is not configured on the local policy path

states.rationale: Leave the Defender Spynet sample-submission policy unset.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems that should keep the normal Defender sample-submission baseline
avoid_for: People explicitly comparing Defender cloud-submission behavior

states.rationale: Keep the policy unset unless there is a clear reason to force sample-submission behavior.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the least-assumptive Defender baseline on this policy surface.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to stop Defender from sending samples during a controlled comparison.
tradeoffs: Does not force a specific sample-submission mode.

recommended_profiles.label: Never send samples
avoid_for: Systems that should keep Defender cloud protection at the normal baseline

states.rationale: Use the documented Never send value that SecurityHealthService.exe read back directly in the 25H2 VM.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to stop Defender sample uploads for a controlled policy comparison.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want Block at First Sight and normal cloud-backed Defender behavior to stay available.
tradeoffs: Stops automatic sample submission on the documented policy surface.
Microsoft documents that this value disables Block at First Sight.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Defender Policy CSP SubmitSamplesConsent
evidence.summary: Microsoft maps SubmitSamplesConsent to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Spynet and documents 0 = Always prompt, 1 = Send safe samples automatically, 2 = Never send, and 3 = Send all samples automatically.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Block at First Sight dependency on sample submission
evidence.summary: Microsoft states that if the sample-submission setting is 2 (Never send), the Block at First Sight feature will not function.

evidence.title: Local Defender sample-submission lead note
evidence.summary: The local lead note tracks SubmitSamplesConsent on HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Spynet with 0, 1, 2, and 3 as the documented values.

evidence.title: Windows Defender dump list includes SubmitSamplesConsent
evidence.summary: The local Defender dump list includes SubmitSamplesConsent under the Defender policy surfaces tracked from upstream registry material.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean absent-value check for Defender sample submission
evidence.summary: In the clean high-risk snapshot run where only SpyNetReporting = 2 was written on the same Defender Spynet policy branch, SecurityHealthService.exe queried SubmitSamplesConsent and got NAME NOT FOUND.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean Procmon read for SubmitSamplesConsent = 2
evidence.summary: After writing SubmitSamplesConsent = 2 on the Defender Spynet policy path, SecurityHealthService.exe queried the same value and read Data: 2 directly from the policy branch.

decision.why: Microsoft documents the exact Defender Spynet path and the 0/1/2/3 value model for SubmitSamplesConsent, and the Win25H2Clean Procmon trace shows SecurityHealthService.exe reading SubmitSamplesConsent = 2 from that path. Value 2 lowers Defender cloud protection, but that tradeoff is documented and explicit rather than an evidence gap.

open_questions: Should the app ever expose the documented default value 1 for comparison, or should it stay on the research-only Never send value?
Do managed Defender environments layer tenant-side overrides on top of the local SubmitSamplesConsent policy in a way the current VM does not show?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: SecurityHealthService.exe | RegQueryValue | HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Spynet\SubmitSamplesConsent | SUCCESS | Type: REG_DWORD, Length: 4, Data: 2
validation_proof.notes: The same clean-snapshot Defender Spynet probe line also showed the sibling SpyNetReporting value as NAME NOT FOUND when SubmitSamplesConsent = 2 was the only policy value on the branch.
